category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Statistics and Probability/Markov Chains and Stochastic Processes

info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Markov Chains and Stochastic Processes

questiontext.text: A discrete-time Markov chain on state space \(S\) satisfies \(P(X_{n+1}=j|X_n=i,X_{n-1},\ldots,X_0)=P(X_{n+1}=j|X_n=i)=p_{ij}\). The matrix \(P=(p_{ij})\) is called the transition matrix. Its defining property is:

answer.text: All entries \(p_{ij}\geq0\) and each row sums to 1 (stochastic matrix)

feedback.text: Correct. A transition matrix is a stochastic matrix: non-negative entries with row sums equal to 1.

answer.text: All entries \(p_{ij}\geq0\) and each column sums to 1

feedback.text: Incorrect. Row sums equal 1 (not column sums) for a stochastic matrix. Column-sum-1 would be doubly stochastic.
Incorrect. General Markov chains need not have symmetric transition matrices.
Incorrect. Stochastic matrices have determinant in \([-1,1]\), not necessarily 1.

questiontext.text: A stationary distribution \(\pi\) of a Markov chain satisfies \(\pi P = \pi\) (row vector) and \(\sum_i\pi_i=1\). For the transition matrix \(P=\begin{pmatrix}0.7&0.3\\0.4&0.6\end{pmatrix}\), the stationary distribution is:

feedback.text: Incorrect. Check: \((1/2)(0.7)+(1/2)(0.4)=0.55\neq1/2\). So \((1/2,1/2)\) is not stationary.
Incorrect. \(\pi P=\pi\) gives \(\pi=(4/7,3/7)\approx(0.571,0.429)\).

questiontext.text: A Markov chain is irreducible if every state communicates with every other state. An irreducible chain on a finite state space has a unique stationary distribution. Aperiodicity (period of every state \(=1\)) plus irreducibility guarantees:

feedback.text: Correct. An irreducible, aperiodic (ergodic) Markov chain on a finite state space converges to the unique stationary distribution from any initial state.

answer.text: The chain is reversible (detailed balance holds)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Irreducibility + aperiodicity gives ergodicity (convergence), not reversibility.
Incorrect. The stationary distribution need not be uniform; it is unique but depends on the transition probabilities.
Incorrect. Aperiodicity means period \(=1\).

questiontext.text: A Markov chain satisfies detailed balance (is reversible) with respect to distribution \(\pi\) if \(\pi_i p_{ij}=\pi_j p_{ji}\) for all \(i,j\). If detailed balance holds, then \(\pi\):

answer.text: Is the stationary distribution of the chain

feedback.text: Correct. Summing \(\pi_ip_{ij}=\pi_jp_{ji}\) over \(i\): \(\sum_i\pi_ip_{ij}=\pi_j\sum_ip_{ji}=\pi_j\), so \(\pi P=\pi\). Detailed balance implies \(\pi\) is stationary.
Incorrect. Detailed balance can hold for non-uniform \(\pi\).
Incorrect. The stationary distribution satisfying detailed balance exists and is unique for irreducible chains.

answer.text: Requires the chain to be periodic

feedback.text: Incorrect. Reversibility (detailed balance) is independent of periodicity.

questiontext.text: In a Markov chain with absorbing states, the absorption probability from transient state \(i\) into absorbing state \(a\) satisfies a linear system. For a simple random walk on \(\{0,1,\ldots,N\}\) (0 and \(N\) absorbing, \(p=q=1/2\)), the probability of absorption at \(N\) starting from \(i\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. For symmetric random walk (gambler's ruin), absorption probability at \(N\) from state \(i\) is \(i/N\).
Incorrect. Absorption at \(N\) depends on starting position: \(P=i/N\).
Incorrect. Absorption probability is \(i/N\) for the symmetric walk.
Incorrect. Absorption probability at \(N\) is linear: \(i/N\).

questiontext.text: For an ergodic Markov chain, the mean return time to state \(i\) (mean first passage time \(m_{ii}\)) satisfies:

feedback.text: Correct. For an ergodic chain, the mean return time to state \(i\) equals the reciprocal of the stationary probability: \(m_{ii}=1/\pi_i\).
Incorrect. The mean return time is \(1/\pi_i\), not \(\pi_i\).
Incorrect. Mean return time \(=1/\pi_i\), which equals \(|S|\) only for the uniform stationary distribution.

answer.text: The mean return time is infinite for all ergodic chains

feedback.text: Incorrect. Ergodic (positive recurrent) chains have finite mean return times.

questiontext.text: In a Galton-Watson branching process, each individual independently produces offspring with PGF \(G(z)=E[z^X]\). The extinction probability \(q\) (probability the population eventually dies out) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The extinction probability \(q\) satisfies \(q=G(q)\) and is the smallest fixed point of \(G\) in \([0,1]\). If mean offspring \(\mu=G'(1)\leq1\), then \(q=1\); if \(\mu>1\), then \(q<1\).
Incorrect. For supercritical processes (\(\mu>1\)), extinction probability \(q<1\) (survival with positive probability).
Incorrect. For subcritical and critical processes (\(\mu\leq1\)), extinction is certain (\(q=1\)).
Incorrect. This formula holds only for Geometric offspring distributions. In general, \(q\) is the smallest root of \(G(q)=q\).

questiontext.text: A Poisson process with rate \(\lambda\) has the property that the number of arrivals in non-overlapping intervals are independent. The waiting time \(W_n\) until the \(n\)-th arrival follows:

feedback.text: Correct. The \(n\)-th arrival time is the sum of \(n\) i.i.d. Exp\((\lambda)\) interarrival times: \(W_n\sim\text{Gamma}(n,1/\lambda)\).
Incorrect. The 1st arrival is Exp\((\lambda)\), but the \(n\)-th is Gamma\((n,1/\lambda)\).
Incorrect. The exact distribution is Gamma; normal is only an approximation for large \(n\).
Incorrect. Waiting times are not uniformly distributed.

questiontext.text: The simple symmetric random walk on \(\mathbb{Z}^d\) (equal probability \(1/(2d)\) to move to each neighbor) is recurrent for \(d=1,2\) and transient for \(d\geq3\). This is known as:

feedback.text: Correct. Pólya proved that the symmetric random walk is recurrent in dimensions 1 and 2 ("you will always return home") but transient in dimension \(\geq3\) ("you might get lost").
Incorrect. The result is Pólya's theorem; Kolmogorov's theorems refer to various other results (extension theorem, 0-1 law, etc.).
Incorrect. de Moivre's theorem is about complex numbers or the CLT for binomials.
Incorrect. Doob's theorems relate to martingales; the random walk recurrence result is Pólya's.

questiontext.text: A birth-death chain on \(\{0,1,2,\ldots\}\) has transition probabilities \(p_{i,i+1}=\lambda_i\) and \(p_{i,i-1}=\mu_i\) (with \(\mu_0=0\)). The stationary distribution satisfies the detailed balance equations. In the M/M/1 queue (\(\lambda_i=\lambda\), \(\mu_i=\mu\)), the stationary distribution is:

feedback.text: Correct. For M/M/1 with traffic intensity \(\rho=\lambda/\mu<1\), the stationary distribution is geometric: \(\pi_n=(1-\rho)\rho^n\).
Incorrect. The M/M/1 stationary distribution is geometric, not Poisson.
Incorrect. The M/M/1 stationary distribution is geometric on all non-negative integers (infinite support).

answer.text: No stationary distribution exists for any \(\rho\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. A stationary distribution exists iff \(\rho=\lambda/\mu<1\) (stable queue).

questiontext.text: A sequence \((M_n, \mathcal{F}_n)\) is a martingale if \(E[M_{n+1}|\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n\). Which of the following is a martingale?

answer.text: \(M_n = S_n - n\mu\) where \(S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\), \(X_i\) i.i.d. with mean \(\mu\)

feedback.text: Correct. \(E[M_{n+1}|\mathcal{F}_n]=E[S_{n+1}-\mu(n+1)|\mathcal{F}_n]=S_n+\mu-(n+1)\mu=S_n-n\mu=M_n\). So \(M_n\) is a martingale.

answer.text: \(M_n = S_n^2\) where \(S_n\) is a simple symmetric random walk

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(E[S_{n+1}^2|\mathcal{F}_n]=S_n^2+1\neq S_n^2\). So \(S_n^2\) is a submartingale; \(S_n^2-n\) is a martingale.
Incorrect. \(E[M_{n+1}|\mathcal{F}_n]=(n+1)^2\neq n^2=M_n\). This is a submartingale.

answer.text: \(M_n = S_n\) where \(S_n\) is a biased random walk with drift \(\mu>0\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(E[S_{n+1}|\mathcal{F}_n]=S_n+\mu>S_n\) for \(\mu>0\). So \(S_n\) is a submartingale, not a martingale.

questiontext.text: Doob's optional stopping theorem states: for a martingale \((M_n)\) and stopping time \(\tau\), \(E[M_\tau]=E[M_0]\) under appropriate conditions. Applying this to the symmetric random walk \(S_n\) and \(\tau=\min\{n:S_n\in\{-a,b\}\}\), we get:

feedback.text: Correct. Since \(E[S_\tau]=0\) (OST applied to the martingale \(S_n\)): \(b\cdot P(S_\tau=b)+(-a)\cdot P(S_\tau=-a)=0\), and \(P+Q=1\). Solving: \(P=a/(a+b)\).
Incorrect. \(P(\text{hit }b)=a/(a+b)\) (depends on distance \(a\) to the lower barrier, not \(b\)).
Incorrect. The probability depends on the relative positions \(a\) and \(b\).
Incorrect. By OST on the martingale, \(E[S_\tau]=E[S_0]=0\).

questiontext.text: Standard Brownian motion \((B_t)_{t\geq0}\) satisfies: \(B_0=0\), independent increments, \(B_t-B_s\sim N(0,t-s)\) for \(s<t\), and continuous paths. Which property holds?

answer.text: \(B_t\) is a martingale, and \(B_t^2-t\) is also a martingale

feedback.text: Correct. \(E[B_t|\mathcal{F}_s]=B_s\) (martingale). \(E[B_t^2-t|\mathcal{F}_s]=E[(B_t-B_s+B_s)^2-t|\mathcal{F}_s]=B_s^2+(t-s)-t+B_s^2... \) simplifies to \(B_s^2-s\). So \(B_t^2-t\) is a martingale.

answer.text: Brownian motion has differentiable paths a.s.

feedback.text: Incorrect. Brownian motion paths are a.s. nowhere differentiable (Paley-Wiener-Zygmund theorem).
Incorrect. \(B_t^2\) is a submartingale; \(B_t^2-t\) is the martingale.

answer.text: Brownian motion has finite variation paths a.s.

feedback.text: Incorrect. Brownian motion has infinite total variation paths a.s. (though finite quadratic variation).

questiontext.text: Let \(\tau_a=\inf\{t\geq0:B_t=a\}\) be the first hitting time of level \(a>0\) for standard Brownian motion. The distribution of \(\tau_a\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The first passage time of BM to level \(a\) has the Lévy (inverse Gaussian with mean \(\infty\)) distribution with density \(f(t)=\frac{a}{\sqrt{2\pi t^3}}e^{-a^2/(2t)}\) for \(t>0\).
Incorrect. The hitting time of BM is Lévy-distributed, not exponential.
Incorrect. The hitting time is non-negative and heavy-tailed (Lévy), not normal.
Incorrect. The hitting time is Lévy-distributed, supported on \((0,\infty)\).

questiontext.text: A stochastic process \((X_t)\) is wide-sense stationary (WSS) if \(E[X_t]=\mu\) (constant) and \(\text{Cov}(X_t,X_{t+h})=R(h)\) depends only on lag \(h\). The power spectral density is the Fourier transform of the autocovariance:

feedback.text: Correct. The Wiener-Khinchin theorem: for a WSS process, the power spectral density is the Fourier transform of the autocovariance function.
Incorrect. The PSD is the Fourier transform of \(R\), not the squared modulus of \(R\) in frequency domain.
Incorrect. This would be the PSD of a constant process; for a WSS process with nontrivial \(R(h)\), the PSD is more complex.
Incorrect. \(R(0)=\text{Var}(X_t)\) is the total power; the PSD distributes this power over frequencies.

questiontext.text: For a Markov chain state \(i\), define \(f_i=P(\text{chain returns to }i|\text{starts at }i)\). State \(i\) is:

answer.text: Recurrent if \(f_i=1\) (certain return), transient if \(f_i<1\) (positive probability of non-return)

feedback.text: Correct. A state is recurrent if \(f_i=1\) (chain returns a.s.) and transient if \(f_i<1\) (positive probability of never returning). Equivalently, state \(i\) is recurrent iff \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty P_{ii}^{(n)}=\infty\).
Incorrect. The definitions are reversed. Recurrent means \(f_i=1\).

answer.text: Recurrent if mean return time is finite

feedback.text: Incorrect. Mean return time being finite is positive recurrence (a stronger condition). Recurrence alone requires only certain return (\(f_i=1\)).

answer.text: Transient if the chain visits \(i\) infinitely often

feedback.text: Incorrect. Infinite visits corresponds to recurrence; transient states are visited only finitely many times.

name.text: STAT20-Q17 Ergodic Theorem for Markov Chains

questiontext.text: For an ergodic Markov chain with stationary distribution \(\pi\), the ergodic theorem states that time averages equal space averages. Specifically, for any bounded function \(g\):

feedback.text: Correct. The ergodic theorem for Markov chains: time averages converge almost surely to the stationary expectation, regardless of the initial state.
Incorrect. Time averages converge to the stationary mean \(E_\pi[g]\), not necessarily 0.

answer.text: Time averages converge to the initial value \(g(X_0)\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. For ergodic chains, time averages converge to the stationary mean, independent of the initial state.

answer.text: Convergence holds only in probability, not almost surely

feedback.text: Incorrect. The ergodic theorem gives almost sure convergence.

questiontext.text: In renewal theory, if inter-renewal times \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) are i.i.d. with mean \(\mu\), the renewal reward theorem states that as \(t\to\infty\), the number of renewals \(N(t)/t\to1/\mu\) a.s. The key elementary renewal theorem gives:

feedback.text: Correct. The elementary renewal theorem: \(\lim_{t\to\infty}E[N(t)]/t=1/\mu\), the long-run renewal rate equals the reciprocal of the mean inter-renewal time.
Incorrect. \(E[N(t)]\approx t/\mu\) for large \(t\), not \(t\mu\).
Incorrect. \(E[N(t)]\to\infty\) as \(t\to\infty\); the rate is \(1/\mu\).
Incorrect. \(N(t)\) is Poisson only for Poisson processes (Exp inter-renewals). For general renewals, \(N(t)\) has a more complex distribution.

name.text: STAT20-Q19 Conditional Independence in Markov Chains

questiontext.text: The Markov property says future is independent of past given present: \(X_{n+1}\perp\!\!\!\perp (X_0,\ldots,X_{n-1})|X_n\). This can be generalized: for \(m<n<k\), which conditional independence holds?

answer.text: \(X_k\perp\!\!\!\perp X_m\mid X_n\) (future independent of past given present)

feedback.text: Correct. The Markov property implies: for \(m<n<k\), the future \(X_k\) is conditionally independent of the past \(X_m\) given the present \(X_n\). This is the global Markov property.
Incorrect. Future and past are not marginally independent; they are only conditionally independent given the present.
Incorrect. Conditioning on a future state does not make the current state independent of a past state.
Incorrect. \(X_k\) depends on the present state \(X_n\).

questiontext.text: The Metropolis-Hastings algorithm constructs a Markov chain with a specified stationary distribution \(\pi\). The acceptance ratio for proposed move from \(x\) to \(y\) (with proposal density \(q(y|x)\)) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio ensures detailed balance with respect to \(\pi\). Gibbs sampling is a special case where \(\alpha=1\).
Incorrect. This is the Metropolis ratio (valid only for symmetric proposals \(q(y|x)=q(x|y)\)).
Incorrect. The acceptance ratio must account for both the target and proposal distributions.
Incorrect. Always accepting would not preserve the target distribution \(\pi\) in general.
